title: Bank Statement Analysis for Convenience Stores
meta: Convenience stores mix card, cash, lottery, and ATM flows. Learn how lenders separate true margin from pass-through and analyze c-store bank statements for funding.
content:
Convenience Store Statements Have the Most Moving Parts

Few small businesses produce a busier bank statement than a convenience store: card batches, cash, lottery, ATM replenishment, money orders, and vendor payments all flow through one or two accounts. Reading them correctly means knowing which flows are revenue and which are pass-through.

How C-Store Money Moves

  • Card settlements: Daily merchant deposits from the in-store POS.
  • ATM vault cash: Large transfers to load the in-store ATM are NOT expenses — they are the store's own cash cycling. A frequent source of underwriter confusion.
  • Lottery and money-order settlements: Pass-through with thin commissions.
  • Cash deposits and vendor ACH: Cigarette and beverage distributors take large, regular payments.

The Pass-Through Problem

Money orders, lottery, and ATM cash inflate both deposits and withdrawals without representing margin. A naive deposit total can overstate revenue by 30% or more. Underwriters must strip pass-through to find the true operating cash that services a loan.

What Lenders Focus On

  • True merchandise margin after pass-through is removed
  • Cigarette/beverage vendor payments staying current
  • NSF and negative-balance days
  • Consistency of daily card settlements

Bottom Line

Convenience stores are fundable, but only after separating pass-through from margin. Automated analysis such as StatementScrub classifies ATM, lottery, and money-order flows so the real number is visible.

Related reading: Gas station analysis | Retail store bank statements | What lenders look for
